name: runtime_script
description: Run one small GDScript inspector or input-driver probe inside an active daemon-managed runtime_session. Call runtime_session.start or runtime_session.status first and pass the returned session_id. The script is saved/diagnosed, sent to the running scene through Fennara's runtime helper, and the tool waits for that script to complete, fail, or time out. Runtime scripts are NOT editor tool scripts: never include `@tool`, and do not copy run_scene_edit_script templates into runtime_script. Scripts must extend RefCounted, define `func run(ctx: Variant) -> void`, use explicit types for all local variables, and use `ctx.log(...)` for observations. Keep runtime logs token-efficient: log compact milestones, sampled state, final summaries, and capture labels; do not log every frame, loop iteration, action press/release, mouse move, or full node dump. In loops, accumulate counters, ranges, a few samples, and goal status, then emit one concise summary. Scripts may complete without closing the scene so follow-up runtime_script calls can inspect or drive the same running scene. Call `ctx.close_scene()` only for the final script when you intentionally want to end the runtime session, or use runtime_session.stop to close it externally. Fennara writes ctx logs, capture events, close requests, runtime errors, and completion markers into the raw runtime_session.log with fixed `FENNARA_SCRIPT_*` prefixes. The runtime_script tool result is an operational receipt: status, session_id, script_run_id, script_path, log path, diagnostics, scene_closed/session_active when available, and capture paths. It is not the source of truth for scene health. Always read runtime_session.log after runtime_session.start, after every runtime_script, and when checking runtime_session.status to understand startup errors, script logs, captures, and runtime errors. Runtime ctx supports log(), wait(), capture(), press_action(), release_action(), tap_action(), action(), action_sequence(), click_at(), click_button(), find_button_by_text(), button_path_by_text(), set_mouse_position(), release_all_actions(), get_scene_root(), get_session_id(), get_script_run_id(), get_captures_dir(), and close_scene(). Await yielding ctx helpers: write `await ctx.wait(...)`, `await ctx.capture(...)`, `await ctx.tap_action(...)`, `await ctx.action(...)`, `await ctx.action_sequence(...)`, `await ctx.click_at(...)`, and `await ctx.click_button(...)`. Without await, scripts can continue immediately, movement/click timing can be wrong, captures may not finish, and observations can be false. Synchronous ctx methods such as ctx.log(), ctx.press_action(), ctx.release_action(), ctx.set_mouse_position(), ctx.release_all_actions(), and getters do not need await. action_sequence steps can include {"action":"move_right","phase":"press"}, {"actions":["move_right","sprint"],"phase":"release"}, {"wait":1.0}, {"capture":"after_jump"}, {"click_at":{"x":640,"y":360}}, {"click_button":"HUD/StartButton"}, and {"mouse_position":{"x":300,"y":200}}. click_button defaults to mouse-style clicking; pass {"mode":"signal"} only when real mouse routing is blocked. For unknown gameplay mechanics, do not assume common genres, objectives, movement semantics, or success conditions. Treat all gameplay semantics as project-local until observed. First run small inspection/probing scripts to discover the live control model and state signals: inspect InputMap action names, controller/player scripts, scene tree, UI, signals, animations, transforms, resources, and current runtime state before driving. Test one primitive input at a time for a short bounded duration, release actions, wait at least one frame, then observe what changed. Infer action effects from measured state deltas, not from action names. Use adaptive observe -> experiment -> infer -> act -> verify loops. Keep experiments bounded by time or iteration count, re-fetch live nodes/state inside loops, and log compact summaries of attempted inputs, observed deltas, inferred effects, chosen next steps, and verified outcomes. If no reliable verification signal is found, report uncertainty and gather more observations instead of claiming success. Do not derive success from intent: pressed input, proximity to a target, clicked coordinates, elapsed time, or suggestive action names are not verification. Verify success from actual runtime state after the game has processed at least one frame, such as a property/counter/UI text change, signal firing, node disappearing or becoming inactive/hidden/disabled, animation/state/scene transition, resource/object count change, or another project-specific state change discovered through inspection. Do not encode genre-specific helpers or assumptions such as navigation, collecting, attacking, jumping, talking, inventory use, pathing, quests, combat, economy, or UI flow. Fennara tools and schemas must remain primitive and game-agnostic: agents should compose low-level inspection, input, timing, capture, and validation primitives for the specific project they are observing. Avoid these runtime_script anti-patterns: holding one guessed action for several seconds without recomputing from live state; assuming InputMap action names map to world directions or common gameplay verbs; incrementing success counters from proximity, attempted input, or elapsed time; keeping stale node references across scene reloads, respawns, or object replacement; logging every frame instead of concise samples and summaries; calling yielding ctx helpers such as wait(), capture(), action(), tap_action(), action_sequence(), click_at(), or click_button() without await; leaving actions pressed after a loop, failure, timeout, or early return.

code.description: Inline GDScript to save, diagnose, and run. Never include `@tool`; runtime_script is not run_scene_edit_script. Must extend RefCounted, define `func run(ctx: Variant) -> void`, and use explicit types for locals such as Node, Node3D, Dictionary, Array[Dictionary], String, float, bool, int, and Vector3. When using yielding ctx helpers, always write `await ctx.wait(...)`, `await ctx.capture(...)`, `await ctx.tap_action(...)`, `await ctx.action(...)`, `await ctx.action_sequence(...)`, `await ctx.click_at(...)`, and `await ctx.click_button(...)`. For gameplay objectives, increment success counters only after verifying real game state changed, not merely because the player reached a position or an input was sent. Omit `ctx.close_scene()` for incremental probes; call it only when this script should close the runtime session. For runtime driving, inspect game code/state first and compose primitive ctx actions; do not rely on assumed meanings like `move_left` = world-left. Pass either `code` or `script_path`, not both.

session_id.description: Required. The active managed runtime session id returned by `runtime_session.start` or `runtime_session.status`.

script_path.description: Existing `res://...gd` runtime script to diagnose and run. Use this to rerun or patch a previously returned script path. Omit `ctx.close_scene()` for incremental probes; call it only when this script should close the runtime session. Pass either `code` or `script_path`, not both.

timeout_ms.description: Optional wait budget for this script result. The scene can stay running after the script completes; use runtime_session.stop or a final script with `ctx.close_scene()` when done.
